Threat.value: Irritable behavior, angry outbursts, or acting aggressively
Having strong physical reactions when something reminded you of the stressful experience

Memory.value: I can talk about what happened very easily.
When I have memories of what happened I sometimes hear things in my head that I heard during the frightening event.
My memories of the frightening event are very clear and detailed.

Negative Appraisals.value: Somebody else would not have gotten into this situation
I have permanently changed for the worse

Maladaptive Strategies.value: keep mind occupied all the time
ruminate about how event could have been prevented
Situations in which other people evaluate me / my appearance / my performance

issues.explanation: Given the trauma type is an accident, it's implausible that people in uniform would be a trigger unless the patient had a specific negative interaction with authority figures during or after the accident. However, there is no supporting evidence in other components to suggest this.

issues.item: Self-Blame: The event happened because of the way I acted
issues.explanation: Accidents are often unforeseen and outside of one's control. Self-blame seems inconsistent with the trauma type unless there is evidence to suggest the patient feels responsible for the accident (e.g., they were driving). There is no supporting evidence in other components.

issues.item: Rumination: Prevention: ruminate about how event could have been prevented
issues.explanation: While it's possible to ruminate about prevention, the patient reports being able to talk about what happened very easily (Verbal Access) and having clear memories of the event (Vivid Memory). These items from the Memory component suggest a level of cognitive processing that seems inconsistent with excessive rumination about prevention.

vignette: At 79 years old, they've lived a life rich in experience and connection, yet a single event has left an indelible mark. The car accident that shook their world still resonates deeply, its impact felt in every aspect of their daily life. Smells reminiscent of the crash – gasoline, smoke, or burnt rubber – transport them back to that fateful day, as if no time has passed at all. Even seeing similar cars on the road can trigger a visceral response, leaving them feeling irritable and on edge. Sleep has become a luxury they can ill afford, with troubles falling and staying asleep a constant companion. Physical reactivity is another unwelcome guest, making its presence known whenever memories of the accident surface. These memories are vivid and unshakeable – auditory recall brings back the cacophony of sounds from that day, while their mind's eye replays every detail in stark relief. Talking about what happened comes easily to them now, a coping mechanism they've developed over time. In quieter moments, self-doubt creeps in, whispering insidious comparisons with others – "somebody else wouldn't have gotten into this situation." A nagging sense of permanent damage has taken hold, eroding their confidence and trust in themselves. They find solace in mental distraction, keeping their mind occupied at all times to avoid those dark corners. Rumination about the accident's preventability is a recurring theme, as they mentally replay every what-if scenario. Avoidance behaviors have become ingrained, especially when it comes to situations where others might evaluate them or their performance. A desire for control and self-protection drives these choices, often at the cost of meaningful connections with loved ones. Their partner has grown increasingly concerned about the distance growing between them, yet they struggle to articulate the fears and anxieties holding them back. The weight of these symptoms is crushing at times, making everyday tasks feel like insurmountable challenges. Despite their resilience and determination, it's clear that this trauma still holds a powerful grip on their life – one they're desperate to break free from, and rediscover a sense of peace and self-assurance.
